Baby Name Trend Visualizer

Add up to 4 names to plot their popularity trends on a single interactive line chart spanning 1880 to today. Filter by country to see how trends differ between the US, UK, Canada and Australia.

Need just two names with a "winner" callout? Use the Name Comparison tool. Looking up one name? Try the Popularity Checker.

Try a preset comparison

Country filter

See how popularity differs by region

How to Read Your Chart — 7-Step Checklist

Follow this quick checklist the first time you use the Trend Visualizer. Each step takes a few seconds and unlocks the full meaning of the chart.

Quick Demo: a typical trend chart

Rising (e.g. Olivia) Vintage revival (e.g. Eleanor) Declining (e.g. Linda)
  • Step 1. Identify the X-axis: decades from 1880s through 2020s, left to right.
  • Step 2. Identify the Y-axis: a 0–100 normalized popularity score (100 = each name's all-time peak).
  • Step 3. Match each colour to a name using the legend at the bottom of the chart.
  • Step 4. Look at the slope: rising line = growing popularity, falling line = declining.
  • Step 5. Find the highest point on each line — that decade is the name's peak.
  • Step 6. Compare line shapes side-by-side: parallel lines = similar trend, crossing lines = trend reversal.
  • Step 7. Hover any data point on desktop (or tap on mobile) to read the exact score.

What is a Baby Name Trend Visualizer?

A baby name trend visualizer is an interactive chart tool that plots multiple names on a single timeline so you can see how each name's popularity has risen, fallen, or stayed flat across decades. The HowManyOfMe visualizer supports up to 4 names simultaneously, draws decade-by-decade lines from 1880 to today using Social Security Administration data, includes a country filter for US/UK/Canada/Australia, and uses normalized 0–100 popularity scores so trend shapes — not absolute counts — are easy to compare. Before diving deep into charts, you can check how many people share your name to get a baseline.

Key Features and Attributes

4-name multi-line chart

Plot up to four names on a single chart with distinct colours for visual comparison.

Country filter (US/UK/CA/AU)

Re-scale the chart by country to see how a name's popularity differs between regions.

One-click presets

Load curated comparison sets (Top Girl Names, Vintage Revival, Mid-Century Classics) instantly.

Interactive tooltips

Hover any point to see the exact popularity score for that name in that decade.

Peak-decade detection

Automatic 'peaked in 1980s' callouts for every name you add.

Mobile-responsive

Chart auto-resizes; tap-and-hold works on iOS and Android.

Who Uses This Tool?

Parents researching name eras

Scenario: Want to see whether 'Eleanor' is genuinely vintage-revival or has stayed steady.

Result: Chart shows Eleanor's revival arc — high in 1910s, dip mid-century, sharp climb post-2010.

Expat parents

Scenario: Living in the UK but considering an American-popular name like 'Mason'.

Result: Switch to the UK filter to see how the name actually performs locally before committing.

Authors building period casts

Scenario: Writing a 4-sibling family in 1990s suburbia.

Result: Add 4 candidate names, keep the ones whose lines peak in the 1980s–90s.

Genealogy hobbyists

Scenario: Comparing 4 ancestor names to see which were 'normal' vs 'unusual' for their era.

Result: Chart instantly reveals which ancestor had the rarest name in their birth decade.

Worked Examples — Try Each in One Click

Click any preset to load it into the chart above and see the full insight rendered live.

Top Girl Names

Names: Emma, Olivia, Sophia, Charlotte

Insight: All four climb steeply post-2000, peaking in the 2010s — the modern girl-name cluster.

Top Boy Names

Names: Liam, Noah, Oliver, Elijah

Insight: All four converge near the top in the 2020s — today's true 'top tier' boy names.

Vintage Revival

Names: Eleanor, Theodore, Hazel, Arthur

Insight: Classic 1880s–1910s names showing strong revival in 2010s+ — the vintage comeback pattern.

Mid-Century Classics

Names: James, Mary, Robert, Linda

Insight: 1940s–1950s peak then decades-long decline — the textbook fading-classic shape.

✓ Pros

  • ✓ Up to 4 names on one chart — best multi-way comparison available
  • ✓ Country filter (US/UK/CA/AU) shows regional differences
  • ✓ One-click presets for common comparison sets
  • ✓ Peak-decade insights generated automatically
  • ✓ Free, unlimited, no signup required
  • ✓ Mobile-responsive interactive chart

✗ Cons

  • ✗ Cap of 4 names — for larger comparisons you'd need a CSV export workflow
  • ✗ Decade granularity (not yearly) means very recent spikes can be smoothed
  • ✗ Country filter currently supports 4 major English-speaking countries

Understanding Baby Name Trends

Baby names reflect culture, history, and generational identity. Over decades, certain names rise dramatically while others fade away. Parents today increasingly rely on data-driven tools to explore historical name popularity before choosing a name for their child.

Names like Emma, Olivia, and Sophia have surged in recent decades while older names such as Gary or Shirley have declined. Visualizing this data is the fastest way to see whether a name is timeless, trending, or fading — and whether it pairs well with a sibling name from the same era. For deeper meaning context, pair this chart with the Name Meaning Lookup.

Why Country Matters

A name's global rank can hide huge regional differences. "Mason" is a top-10 boy name in the US but barely cracks the UK top 100. The country filter on this chart re-scales each line to that country's share of bearers, so you see the local trend — essential for expat parents and authors writing region-specific characters.

Frequently Asked Questions

How accurate is baby name popularity data?▾

Our underlying data comes from official birth registries — primarily the U.S. Social Security Administration (1880–present), the UK ONS, and 78 other national statistical agencies — making it as accurate as the source records themselves.

How many names can be compared on the Trend Visualizer?▾

Up to four names can be plotted on a single chart simultaneously, each with its own colour line. For two-name head-to-head views, use the Name Comparison tool.

How does the country filter work?▾

Switching from Global to US, UK, Canada or Australia rescales each name's decade score by that country's historical share of the name's total bearers. A name that's globally popular but US-rare will look different on the US filter than on Global.

Why are trends shown by decade?▾

Decade grouping smooths year-to-year noise and reveals long-term cultural patterns — generational revivals, slow declines, and breakout decades become immediately visible.

What does the 0–100 popularity score mean?▾

It's a normalized rank score: 100 represents the decade in which the name reached its all-time peak, and every other decade is scaled relative to that peak.

Can I export the chart?▾

Right-click any chart to save it as an image. We're working on a one-click PNG/CSV export — coming soon.

Does the visualizer work on mobile?▾

Yes — the chart auto-resizes responsively and supports touch tooltips on iOS and Android.

Why is my name not showing data?▾

Names with fewer than 5 recorded bearers in any decade fall below our display threshold. Try checking the spelling or a common alternate (e.g. 'Catherine' vs 'Katherine').

How often is the dataset updated?▾

Annually, every January, with the previous calendar year's birth registry release.

Is the Trend Visualizer free?▾

Yes. Unlimited charts, no signup, no email, no paywall.

Rising Names

Names showing consistent decade-over-decade growth — look for upward-sloping lines.

Declining Names

Once-popular names now fading — high left side, low right side on the chart.

Timeless Names

Roughly flat lines — names that stay stable across generations.
